All reports
Postal & Copy Plus
Postal & Copy Plus

Voice of Customer Report

What print buyers say in their own words, and what their words tell us to do.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Print buyers in this category keep asking for three things: color and quality that match what they approved, a human who owns the problem when an order goes wrong, and a refund rather than a store credit that locks them back in. The biggest gap is that online printers sell speed and price while buyers, after they have been burned, talk about trust and accountability. The highest-value language shift moves the message off fast-and-cheap and onto the person who answers and a print the buyer can stand behind.

Three wants run through every platform, in the buyers’ own language.

They want the print to come out as approved. One buyer’s color complaint is exact: “CYMK designs printed far darker and far less saturated than the designs.”1 Another’s image “‘fall off’ the card. The colors were very off too (greenish - bleh!)”.2

They want a person who owns it. The praise that converts buyers into advocates names people: “Thank you, Mark, Stephanie, Cindy and the team!”3 The complaints name the absence of one: “to call and speak to a new person every time!”4

They want their money back, not a credit. The single most repeated structural complaint is the store-credit trap: “they offered me an online credit, which is useless to me since I wont be ordering again.”5

This evidence base is built from public reviews of the client’s competitors and the category. Section 0.5 states that plainly, and Section 8 makes commissioning a primary-interview pass the first recommended move.

0.5 Evidence Base and Recommended Primary Research

This report is built entirely from public sources. There were no first-party customer interviews in this engagement; the voice of the customer here is assembled from public reviews of the online giants (Vistaprint, Custom Ink, Printful, GotPrint, Signs.com, Staples print), from category forums where buyers debate local print shops against online ones, and from the three public reviews of the client’s own storefront, Postal and Copy Plus.6

That provenance is a strength for direction and a limit on precision. Across hundreds of public reviews the patterns are consistent enough to act on: the same color failures, the same store-credit traps, the same hunger for a named human appear on Sitejabber, ConsumerAffairs, BBB, the Shopify App Store, and the forums alike. What public reviews cannot give is the client’s own win/loss reasons, the size of the defecting segment in the Clarkston and Lewiston trade area, or the words the client’s actual walk-in buyers use at the counter.

The client’s own public voice is thin: three short reviews, all positive, all about service (“Great, friendly service”,7 “Willing to help in anyway possible. Great customer service”,8 “Friendly staff and nice environment”9). The signal points the right way. It is too small to carry a strategy by itself.

A primary-interview pass would confirm or sharpen four things this report can only infer from competitors’ customers: the exact emotional registers (resignation, vindication, relief) the client’s own buyers carry; the buyer-versus-driver split (who pays versus who hands out the cards); the real Jobs-to-be-Done timeline of a local buyer choosing the counter over the giants; and the win/loss forces that pull a defecting buyer in or keep them with the incumbent. Section 8 makes this the first move.

1. How Customers Actually Talk About Print

The category voice is remarkably uniform. The same complaints recur across vendors that compete on price and scale, and the same praise recurs wherever a human takes ownership. Five patterns carry the most weight for messaging.

“the colors were very off”

The most common quality failure is color and registration, and buyers describe it in physical, embarrassing detail. This is category-wide: it lands on Vistaprint, GotPrint, and the broader online field alike.

“Used Vistaprint for years. Something changed. Last 5 orders arrived in horrible quality. CYMK designs printed far darker and far less saturated than the designs. They reran the cards knowing the issue, and sent the same garbage round two. Use Moo.”1 Persona: Repeat small-business buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“literally a THIRD of my 500 cards was completely unusable because they ‘drifted’ or something in the printing process, making the whole image kinda ‘fall off’ the card. The colors were very off too (greenish - bleh!), and I was so embarrassed to give them to anyone. The whole thing was only like $17, so I can’t complain too bad, but for my needs I won’t be using them again.”2 Persona: Solo maker / small-shop owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“All of the cards are printed off center and the matte paper upgrade option feels like something I could have printed at home. The edges are not smooth and the cards are very thin. To be honest, this company feels like a complete scam after this interaction. Print junk and then offer a store credit to do it all over again. AVOID THEM!”10 Persona: Quality-sensitive buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The word buyers reach for is “embarrassed.” The print is going to be handed to someone, and a bad print embarrasses the person handing it over. To these buyers, color accuracy is whether they can give the thing away with pride.

“call and speak to a new person every time”

Buyers describe faceless support as its own injury, separate from the print failure. This pattern is heaviest on Custom Ink, where buyers name the churn of anonymous reps directly.

“When Custom Ink gets it right, they do great work. But getting there can be real frustrating and aggravating. And to call and speak to a new person every time! I’m done with Custom Ink. I’ve had better luck with the ‘Mom and Pop’ T-Shirt companies.”4 Persona: Repeat apparel buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I understand this was a small order, but it feels like Custom Ink simply doesn’t care unless you’re spending big money. I won’t be using them again, and I wouldn’t recommend them to anyone who values reliability, responsiveness, or basic customer service.”11 Persona: Small-order event buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Local shops will work with you and if you need changes, additional orders, add-ons, etc. you have a much better chance of them working with you. If you need any of those with this site, then you will have to start from scratch each time.”12 Persona: Repeat customization buyer. Stage: Evaluation.

“Start from scratch each time” is the structural complaint underneath the emotional one. The online model has no memory of the buyer, and buyers feel it as not being cared about.

“they offered me an online credit, which is useless to me”

When an order goes wrong, the moment that turns a disappointed buyer into an ex-customer is the resolution offer. A store credit reads as a trap, not a remedy. This appears on GotPrint and the online field generally.

“When I contacted customer service, I explained that I didnt have time to reorder and requested a refund. Instead, they offered me an online credit, which is useless to me since I wont be ordering again.”5 Persona: One-off project buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I’ve had issues with prints in the past but still stuck with VistaPrint. They would not replace them for me or send me a refund. I’m shocked. I’m going to have to search for a new printing company after all these years.”13 Persona: Long-tenured loyal buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Very poor quality. They will not make their product right. They will blame you for not sending a high quality logo. They will send you some terrible quality printed merchandise and refuse to make it right, you’re stuck with what they send you. Lesson learned here.”14 Persona: Small-business buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

“Stuck with what they send you” and “useless to me” are the same feeling: the remedy keeps the buyer tied to the vendor that already failed them. The refund-versus-credit choice is where loyalty is actually lost.

“they blew my deadline, didnt tell me”

The deadline failure is its own theme because the silence around it is what stings. Buyers plan launches and events around a promised date, and the missed date arrives without warning. This runs across GotPrint, Signs.com, and Staples print.

“They blew my deadline, didnt tell me, and wouldnt refund the cost of the job. Im a professional designer. Ill never use them again.”15 Persona: Professional designer / pro buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“The start of my Business was planned around getting these cards to have and hand out. Instead I had to write my number down on ripped up pieces of paper. Thanks a whole lot GotPrint.”16 Persona: New business owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I placed an order for three items and paid for premium shipping because the Staples website clearly stated that the products would be printed within 24 hours. 48 hours after ordering, I received another email stating that the notebook I requested was not in stock. my event was negatively impacted, and the opportunity to present the materials was lost.”17 Persona: Event / deadline buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The injury is being left to find out on their own, and being told too late to fix it. The lateness alone would sting less.

“Especially for lower volumes”

Underneath all of it sits a price argument that runs in both directions, and it is the one place buyers actively defend the online giants against local shops. Low-volume buyers say local pricing punishes them.

“Prices at local places are a joke. Especially for lower volumes.”18 Persona: Price-driven low-volume buyer. Stage: Evaluation.
“There are dozens of places who print these signs for far less - don’t let the promise of expedited delivery fool you!”19 Persona: Price-shopping signage buyer. Stage: Evaluation.
“As a buyer I don’t pay much attention to what a business card looks like. The business card itself is not what sells me on a shop, it’s the quality of the item and the customer service.”20 Persona: Low-involvement commodity buyer. Stage: Evaluation.

This counter-segment is large. A meaningful share of buyers want functional and cheap and will leave the instant a local shop charges more. The opening sits with a different group: the buyers who have already been burned by cheap and now weigh quality and accountability above price.

2. The Emotional Undercurrent

The emotions in these reviews are worth naming, because they tell a copywriter which feeling to speak to.

Embarrassment is the dominant emotion around quality. Buyers describe being “so embarrassed to give them to anyone”2 when color drifts. The product is social, and a bad print is a small public failure.

Resignation hardening into defection runs through the long-tenured buyers. The tone stays weary: “I’ve had issues with prints in the past but still stuck with VistaPrint… I’m going to have to search for a new printing company after all these years.”13 The six-year loyalist’s weariness is its own register.

“I am so sick of dealing with this; it has cost me over $100+. As a loyal customer for over six years, it is absolutely ridiculous to be dealing with this now.”21 Persona: Six-year loyal buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Betrayal and the sense of a trap surface in the word buyers reach for when the resolution process feels designed to keep their money: scam. “this company feels like a complete scam after this interaction. Print junk and then offer a store credit to do it all over again.”10 The reseller’s all-caps anger lands the same way.

“when you doing business with printful it feels like youre getting SCAM OR ROB. I try their subscription $24.99 for all these perks and come to find out you pay more then the ‘free plan’. NOT COOL!!!!!”22 Persona: Long-tenured reseller. Stage: Use & Succeed.
  • Relief and gratitude, naming a person, is the strongest positive register, and it is the emotion the client should aim to produce. A 2:30 AM rescue of a 5,000-card order earned this.
“They already had me as a customer for life but now, I will Advocate for life!”23 Persona: High-volume business buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I don’t enjoy online usually. I appreciate that you have a phone number that reached a member of your team. In both calls the individual was very helpful and it was a pleasure to have reordered from you!”24 Persona: Phone-preferring / online-averse buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The buyer-versus-user split

The economic buyer and the end user often want opposite things, and the split should drive how the client segments its messaging.

The end user who hands out the cards, or hands them to a client, is driven by pride and the fear of embarrassment. Their language is physical and emotional: cards that are “meant to be kept”, that “you can’t tear it up if you wanted it, they have to be cut.”25 For this buyer the print is an extension of their own reputation.

“your card should be so good that even if they don’t like you they won’t throw it out. my cards are meant to be kept. you can’t tear it up if you wanted it, they have to be cut. [Moo Luxe cards] are 6x times as thick [as] vista print cards.”25 Persona: Premium brand-identity buyer. Stage: Evaluation.

The economic buyer who signs off on cost is driven by total price and the fear of a hidden charge. Their language is about dollars and surprises: an “exorbitant $351” shipping charge on a 6oz board,26 and a $132 charge that drew “Total overcharge!!!!”27.

“It took 10 days to complete a very simple design that was originally quoted at 2-3 days. only after moving forward with the order did I learn that the shipping cost for a single 36x48 foam board (weighing only 6oz) would be an exorbitant $351 - for ground shipping, not expedited. This charge is unreasonable and should have been disclosed up front before the order was finalized.”26 Persona: Occasional signage buyer. Stage: Purchase.
“Ordered a copy of a blouse pattern to be made on 2 sheets of 47" X 36" sheets of paper. They charged me $132!!! Never again! Total overcharge!!!! Will not return.”27 Persona: One-off large-format buyer. Stage: Purchase.

The messaging consequence is direct. Sell pride and keepability to the end user. Sell a clear all-in price with no surprise at checkout to the economic buyer. The same headline cannot do both, so the storefront should speak to each at the point where each decides.

3. Why They Hired

The path to choosing a printer runs from a first thought to a settled habit. The customer voice marks each stage.

First thought and research. The trigger is a job: business cards for a launch, signs for an event, prints to resell. The first move is a search, and the first feeling is wariness of unknown vendors.

“The local print shop wants an arm an a leg for glossy business cards. Googling brings up tons of what seems like sweet deals. But I’ve never heard of any of the companies. I’m not sure how to go about properly proofing my design. Don’t want to pay for 2,000 cards and get something that doesn’t look like I envisioned it.”28 Persona: First-time online print buyer. Stage: Research.

Passive and active evaluation. Buyers weigh local against online, hands-on against cheap. The quality-first buyer talks themselves toward paying more. The hands-on buyer talks themselves toward local.

“Maybe it might be a better idea to go to the print press guy up the street from me and pay more but I can be hands on with him and get exactly the cards I want.”30 Persona: First-time print buyer weighing local vs online. Stage: Evaluation.
“perfect feedback, overnightprints is quite a bit more expensive, but quality is my #1 thing.”29 Persona: Quality-first buyer. Stage: Evaluation.

Decision and first use. The buyer who could print at home decides a pro is worth it for resale-grade output. A shop operator describes the behavior plainly.

“We have a lot of customers that have printers at home, but still choose to use us for their high end prints that they offer for sale, because our prints turn out a lot more vibrant and detailed than theirs at home do. it may be worth the extra money to let them do it professionally.”31 Persona: Quality-seeking seller who could DIY. Stage: Evaluation.

Habit. The buyer who stays is the one for whom the shop bundles speed, price, and quality, and who stops shopping around. The local-shop loyalist puts the lock-in on service.

“Been using Signs for years. They are quick, the cost is very reasonable and the quality is excellent. Highly recommend them for any services.”32 Persona: Multi-year repeat buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I’m starting to branch into larger prints, which I’ve gotten through them, and they’ve looked really great, prices are good, and you can’t beat the customer service. Just ordered more from them last night :)”33 Persona: Repeat local-print-shop buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

4. Why They Almost Didn’t

The forces that keep a buyer from progressing show up as the push out of their current vendor, the pull toward a new one, the anxiety that freezes the decision, and the habit that holds them in place. Named in the buyers’ own words, these are the objections a storefront has to answer.

Push (what drives them away from the incumbent). Repeated quality failures and a vendor that will not make it right. “They reran the cards knowing the issue, and sent the same garbage round two.”1 The faceless rep: “to call and speak to a new person every time!”4

Pull (what draws them to the alternative). A named human who owns the problem, and the chance to be hands-on. The hands-on draw of local is its own pull: “I can be hands on with him and get exactly the cards I want.”30

“the follow-up email wasn’t just a formal note, they addressed the issue immediately and free of charge. Thank you, Mark, Stephanie, Cindy and the team!”3 Persona: Repeat business buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Anxiety (what makes them hesitate before buying). Fear of paying for a large run that misses what they pictured, and fear of a hidden charge sprung at checkout. The experienced buyer’s specific dread is color that lands “a little off from what I originally planned.”34

“I’ve used overnightprints before. They have good prices, but sometimes I have color issues. they end up being a little off from what I originally planned, while vista doesn’t usually have that problem.”34 Persona: Experienced online print buyer. Stage: Evaluation.
“They ruined the gift, and didn’t offer to make real amends. I really wish I had gone with another outfit.”35 Persona: Occasion / gift buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Habit (what holds them with the incumbent). Switching costs and stored assets keep buyers in place even when they are unhappy. One merchant’s design files survived a five-year gap and held them to the platform.

“We had a failed partnership with our designer, and we logged back in almost 5 years later, and everything we worked so hard to create was still in Printful. Completely saved the business as all our design files were in Printful. we will never go anywhere else.”36 Persona: Established merchant. Stage: Use & Succeed.

5. The Language Gap

Customers and vendors describe the same category in different vocabularies. The vendor column below is the category cliche heard across online printers generally, never the client’s own copy. The shift column is the language to move toward.

What customers say (verbatim) What vendors say (category cliche) The shift to make
“the colors were very off too (greenish - bleh!)”2 ‘vibrant, true-to-screen color’ Promise the proof, not the adjective: show an approved-proof guarantee and color match on the page
“to call and speak to a new person every time!”4 ‘24/7 customer support’ Name the person who owns the order, with a photo, not a support queue
“they offered me an online credit, which is useless to me”5 ‘100% satisfaction guarantee’ Say ‘full refund or a free reprint, your choice,’ and put it where buyers look
“They blew my deadline, didnt tell me”15 ‘fast turnaround / same-day’ Commit to a date and to telling them the moment it slips
“the shipping cost… would be an exorbitant $351”26 ‘free shipping on orders over $X’ Show the all-in price before checkout, no surprise charge
“I can be hands on with him and get exactly the cards I want”30 “easy online design tool” Offer in-person proofing and local pickup as the headline, not a footnote

Words to adopt: owns it, made it right, full refund or free reprint, your choice, approved proof, all-in price, hands-on, same-day pickup, a person who answers, embarrassed (name the feeling and then remove it).

Words to drop: seamless, satisfaction guaranteed, world-class support, fast turnaround (unqualified), premium quality (unqualified), 24/7 support, easy online ordering.

6. What Customers Feel But Do Not Say

These are inferred only from the patterns the quotes support. They are the unspoken needs a storefront can answer before a buyer asks.

No buyer writes that they fear being humiliated, yet the word “embarrassed” and the care about cards that are “meant to be kept”25 show the fear is social before it is technical. The unvoiced need is to be protected from looking unprofessional.

Buyers rarely ask for a single point of contact in those words, yet “speak to a new person every time”4 and “start from scratch each time”12 together show they want continuity: one person who remembers the order. The unvoiced need is an ongoing relationship rather than a one-off transaction.

No buyer spells out that a credit feels like a trap, yet the repeated pairing of “refund” against “online credit”5 shows they read store credit as a vendor protecting itself. The unvoiced need is to be released, and that release is what builds the trust to come back.

No buyer asks to be told the bad news early, yet the sting in “didnt tell me”15 shows a proactive heads-up on a slipping deadline would defuse most of the anger. The unvoiced need is honesty about problems, ahead of the problem landing.

7. Rave Notes

A harvestable testimonial bank, grouped by theme. Every quote is cited to its source. These are the strongest advocacy verbatims for a copywriter to pull from when writing proof points and social proof. They come from competitors’ reviews and the client’s own; use them as evidence of what wins in the category, and as a template for the testimonials the client should collect from its own buyers.

A human who owns the problem

“the follow-up email wasn’t just a formal note, they addressed the issue immediately and free of charge. Thank you, Mark, Stephanie, Cindy and the team!”3 Persona: Repeat business buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“They already had me as a customer for life but now, I will Advocate for life!”23 Persona: High-volume business buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I don’t enjoy online usually. I appreciate that you have a phone number that reached a member of your team. In both calls the individual was very helpful and it was a pleasure to have reordered from you!”24 Persona: Phone-preferring / online-averse buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Service worth coming back for

“It’s an amazing staff at Print & marketing. Really appreciate the help at self serve. Grace helped us and is an amazing. I am happy because she explained everything and she know how to work on phone and all.”37 Persona: Help-needing walk-in buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I’m starting to branch into larger prints, which I’ve gotten through them, and they’ve looked really great, prices are good, and you can’t beat the customer service. Just ordered more from them last night :)”33 Persona: Repeat local-print-shop buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Speed, price, and quality together

“Been using Signs for years. They are quick, the cost is very reasonable and the quality is excellent. Highly recommend them for any services.”32 Persona: Multi-year repeat buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The client’s own buyers

“I use Postal & Copy for all of my companies printing needs and would recommend it to anyone! Great, friendly service. …. especially compared to some other print stores in town. And the huckleberry stuff is a bonus!!”7 Persona: Local B2B repeat customer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Willing to help in anyway possible. Great customer service.”8 Persona: Local walk-in customer. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Friendly staff and nice environment also cute items in their store”9 Persona: Local walk-in customer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

8. Strategic Implications

Each implication translates the customer voice into a move and ties it to the company’s source of truth: lead with local human accountability and same-day service, the one thing the online giants structurally cede. The job is to surface that truth where the market can see it. The reviews show where to aim the perception work, they do not redefine the company.

1. Commission a primary-interview and case-study program. This report runs on competitors’ reviews, not the client’s own customers. Commission an AI-led custom interview pass with recent Postal and Copy Plus buyers and a sample of local defectors from the giants, to confirm and sharpen the emotional registers, the buyer-versus-driver split, the Jobs-to-be-Done timeline, and the win/loss forces named above. Run it before scaling the storefront so the messaging rests on the client’s own buyers. Commission it first.

  • 2. Make a named, photographed human the centerpiece of every order. Buyers’ sharpest complaint is “to call and speak to a new person every time!”4 and their warmest praise names people: “Thank you, Mark, Stephanie, Cindy and the team!”3 Put the accountable person’s name and photo on every order confirmation and on the storefront, and answer the phone. This operationalizes the local-human-accountability source of truth and supports the Brief’s reposition-to-lead recommendation. Surface the person.

3. Replace the store-credit trap with a stated refund-or-reprint choice. The defection moment is the credit offer: “they offered me an online credit, which is useless to me.”5 Publish a no-caveats guarantee that gives the buyer a full refund or a free reprint, their choice, and position it explicitly against the giants’ credit traps. Build it only as far as the shop can honor it. Publish the guarantee.

  1. 4. Show the all-in price and the approved proof before checkout. Buyers fear the hidden charge of an “exorbitant $351”26 and the print that misses what they pictured, “something that doesn’t look like I envisioned it.”28 Surface the total cost and an approved-proof step in the flow, and make in-person proofing and same-day pickup a headline, not a footnote. Deploy proof-and-price-before-checkout.
  2. 5. Speak to the buyer and the user separately. The end user wants pride and a keepable card. The economic buyer wants a clear price with no surprise at checkout. Segment the storefront copy so each meets its own decision driver at the right point, rather than one headline trying to carry both. Split the messaging.
  3. 6. Treat the review surface as a sales floor. Prospects are reading public reviews mid-evaluation: the first-time buyer says “I’ve never heard of any of the companies”28 and resolves the doubt by reading what others experienced, and the category’s most-cited proof points are reviews naming people and rescues. Stand up active review collection and response on the platforms buyers actually read, and seed it with the testimonials the primary-interview pass produces. Own the review floor.

Sources

  • ConsumerAffairs, Vistaprint, 1-star reviewer, Dec 6 2025, https://www.consumeraffairs.com/online/vistaprint.html
  • Etsy Success community forum, Vistaprint (MOO vs Vistaprint thread), reviewer Leslie, https://community.etsy.com/t5/Etsy-Success/moo-com-vs-Vistaprint-worth-it/m-p/119187607

  • Sitejabber, Signs.com, reviewer Michael S. (New York), Sep 2 2025, https://www.sitejabber.com/reviews/signs.com

Postal & Copy Plus
